content: Which of the following cell was used in Apollo space programme?
explanation: It was chosen for its ability to generate electricity by combining hydrogen and oxygen from hydrogen peroxide, producing water as a byproduct. This type of fuel cell provided a reliable and efficient source of power for the spacecraft during the Apollo missions.

content: Dilution affects both conductivity as well as molar conductivity. Effect of dilution on both is as follows:

options.content: conductivity increases whereas molar conductivity decreases on dilution.
conductivity decreases whereas molar conductivity increases on dilution.

explanation: Conductivity depends on the concentration of ions per unit volume of the solution. On dilution this decreases. Molar conductivity increases because with dilution the dissociation of the weak electrolyte increases, causing an increase in the number of active ions in solution.

content:
A voltaic cell is made by connecting two half cells represented by half equations below:

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Sn}_{(a q)}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Sn}_{(\mathrm{s})} \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=-0.14 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}{ }_{(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}{ }_{(\mathrm{aq})} \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

Which statement is correct about this voltaic cell?

options.content: $$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$$ is oxidised and the voltage of the cell is $$-0.91 \mathrm{~V}$$
$$\mathrm{Sn}$$ is oxidised and the voltage of the cell is $$0.91 \mathrm{~V}$$
$$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$$ is oxidised and the voltage of the cell is $$0.91 \mathrm{~V}$$
$$\mathrm{Sn}$$ is oxidised and the voltage of the cell is $$0.63 \mathrm{~V}$$

explanation:
$$\mathrm{Sn}$$ is oxidized as it is having $$-$$ve value of electrode potential.

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{c}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} \\ & =0.77(-0.14) \\ & =0.91 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Four half reactions I to IV are shown below:

I. $$2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_2+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$$

II. $$4 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$$

III. $$\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}$$

IV. $$2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2$$

Which two of these reactions are most likely to occur when concentrated brine is electrolysed?
explanation:
(1) At anode $$2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_2+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$$

(2) At cathode

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{o}}=-2.71 \mathrm{~V} \\ & 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{o}}=0 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

Those have high $$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{o}}$$ value, that reaction will take place.

content: Which of the following cell was used in Apollo space programme?
explanation: $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$$ fuel cell is used in apollo space programme to provide electricity power.

content:
State:

(a) Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions.

(b) Faraday's first law of electrolysis.
explanation:
(a) Kohlrausch's law states that the equivalent conductivity of an electrolyte at infinite dilution is equal to the sum of the conductance of the anions and cations.

(b) Faraday's first law of electrolysis states, during electrolysis, the amount of substance produced at any electrode as a result of chemical reaction proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

content: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with 0.2 mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution is $200 \Omega$. If the resistance of the same cell when filled with $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution is 620 , calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution. The conductivity of 0.2 $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution is $0.0248 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { Cell constant } & =\text { conductivity } \times \text { resistance } \\ & =0.0248 \times 200=4.96 \mathrm{~m}^{-1} \\ & =0.0496 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { For } 0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{KCl} \text { solution: }\\ &\begin{aligned} \text { Cell constant } & =\text { conductivity } \times \text { resistance } \\ \text { Conductivity } & =\text { cell constant } / \text { resistance } \\ \text { Conductivity } & =\frac{0.0496}{620} \\ & =8 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \\ \text { Concentration } & =0.05 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} \\ \text { Molar conductivity } & =\frac{\text { conductivity }}{\text { concentration }} \\ & =\frac{8 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =160 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Calculate the emf of the following cell:

$$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{~s})$$

Given that $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=1.05 \mathrm{~V}, \log 10=1$
explanation:
Using the Nernst equation, you can calculate the emf of the cell. The Nernst equation is:

$ \mathrm{E}_{\text{cell}} = \mathrm{E}_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Products}]}{[\text{Reactants}]} $

For this cell, the half-reactions involve 2 electrons. Therefore, $ n = 2 $.

Given:

$\mathrm{E}_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} = 1.05\, \mathrm{V}$

$[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}] = 0.1\, \mathrm{M}$

$[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}] = 0.01\, \mathrm{M}$

Substitute these values into the Nernst equation:

$ \mathrm{E}_{\text{cell}} = 1.05 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}]}{[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}]^2} $

$ = 1.05 - 0.0296 \log \frac{0.1}{(0.01)^2} $

Calculate the concentration ratio:

$ = 1.05 - 0.0296 \log 10^3 $

Given $\log 10 = 1$, it follows that $\log 10^3 = 3$.

Now, substitute:

$ = 1.05 - 0.0296 \times 3 $

$ = 1.05 - 0.0888 $

Finally, calculate the emf:

$ \mathrm{E}_{\text{cell}} = 0.9612\, \mathrm{V} $

Thus, the emf of the cell is approximately $0.9612\, \mathrm{V}$.

content:
(a) (i) What should be the signs (positive/ negative) for $$\mathrm{E}_{\text {Cell }}^0$$ and $$\Delta \mathrm{G}^0$$ for a spontaneous redox reaction occurring under standard conditions?

(ii) State Faraday's first law of electrolysis.

OR

(b) Calculate the emf of the following cell at $$298 \mathrm{~K}$$ :

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Fe}_{(\mathrm{s})}\left|\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{H}_{(1 \mathrm{M})}^{+}\right| \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}(1 \mathrm{bar}), \mathrm{Pt}_{(\mathrm{s})} \\ & \text { Given } \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cen}}^0=0.44 \mathrm{~V}. \end{aligned}$$
explanation:
(a) (i) $$\left.\begin{array}{c}\Delta \mathrm{G}^0=-\mathrm{ve} \\ \mathrm{E}^0 \text { cell }=+\mathrm{ve}\end{array}\right]$$ for spon tan eous reaction

(ii) Faraday's $$^{\prime+s}$$ first law of electrolysis - The amount of chemical reaction which occur at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte (solution).

OR

(b) According to the equation

$$\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$$

By applying nearest Equation-

$$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =0.44-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{(1)} \\ & =0.44-0.0295 \log 10^{-3} \\ & =0.44-0.0295(-3 \log 10) \text { as, }(\log 10=1) \\ & =0.44+0.089 \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0.53 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Rahul set-up an experiment to find resistance of aqueous $$\mathrm{KCl}$$ solution for different concentrations at $$298 \mathrm{~K}$$ using a conductivity cell connected to a Wheatstone bridge. He fed the Wheatstone bridge with a.c. power in the audio frequency range 550 to 5000 cycles per second. Once the resistance was calculated from null point he also calculated the conductivity $$K$$ and molar conductivity $$\wedge_m$$ and recorded his readings in tabular form.

S.No. Conc. (M) $$\mathrm{k~S~cm^{-1}}$$ $$\mathrm{\wedge_m~S~cm^2~mol^{-1}}$$
1. $$1.00$$ $$111.3\times10^{-3}$$ $$111.3$$
2. $$0.10$$ $$12.9\times10^{-3}$$ $$129.0$$
3. $$0.01$$ $$1.41\times10^{-3}$$ $$141.0$$

Answer the following questions:

(a) Why does conductivity decrease with dilution?

(b) If $$\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{ }^0$$ of $$\mathrm{KCl}$$ is $$150.0 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$, calculate the degree of dissociation of $$0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$$.

(c) If Rahul had used $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ instead to $$\mathrm{KCl}$$ then would you except the $$\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}$$ values to be more or less than those per $$\mathrm{KCl}$$ for a given concentration. Justify.

OR

(c) Amit, a classmate of Rahul repeated the same experiment with $$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}$$ solution instead of $$\mathrm{KCl}$$ solution. Give one point that would be similar and one that would be different in his observations as compared to Rahul.
explanation:
(a) Conductivity decreases with dilution because it depends upon the number of ions present in the solution. When dilution increases number of available ions decreases. Hence, conductivity decreases.

(b)

$$\begin{aligned} & \propto=\frac{i_m^{\circ}}{i_m} \\ & i_m=150.0 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & i_m^{\circ}=141.0 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \propto=\frac{141}{150}=0.94 \end{aligned}$$

(c) Molar conductivity of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ will be high because when it break down into ions, it produce

$$\mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$$

Its $$\mathrm{H}^{+}$$ (cation) size is smaller then $$\mathrm{K}^{+}$$ ion. So for same concentration of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ and $$\mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{HCl}$$ shows high molar conductivity.

[OR]

(c) (i) $$\mathrm{KCl}$$ is strong electrolyte and completely dissociate into their respective ion while $$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COOH}$$ is weak electrolyte and do not completely dissociate.

(ii) Number of ions produced after dissociation are equal

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{KCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\ & \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COO}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \end{aligned}$$
